questions.question: Define a simple random sample in the context of sampling attributes from a population. Why is this method important for statistical inference?
questions.modelAnswer: A simple random sample is a subset of a population where every member has an equal probability of being selected. This method is crucial for statistical inference because it helps to avoid selection bias and ensures the sample is representative, allowing results to be generalized to the population with known probabilities.

questions.question: Describe how the sample proportion is used as a point estimate for a population proportion. Include the formula for calculating the sample proportion and discuss one key property of this estimator.
questions.modelAnswer: The sample proportion (p̂) serves as a point estimate for the true, unknown population proportion (p). It is calculated as p̂ = x/n, where x is the number of successes in the sample and n is the sample size. A key property of p̂ is that it is an unbiased estimator, meaning the expected value of the sampling distribution of p̂ is equal to the population parameter p.

questions.question: Explain the process of constructing a confidence interval for a population proportion. Your answer should detail the necessary assumptions, the formula used, and how to interpret the resulting interval in the context of a real-world problem, such as estimating the failure rate of a new computer component.
questions.modelAnswer: Constructing a confidence interval for a proportion requires a random sample and a sample size large enough so that both np̂ and n(1-p̂) are greater than 5, ensuring the sampling distribution is approximately normal. The formula is p̂ ± z*√(p̂(1-p̂)/n), where z is the z-score for the desired confidence level. For example, if surveying 500 components and finding 20 failures (p̂=0.04), a 95% CI might be 0.04 ± 1.96*√(0.04*0.96/500). We interpret this as being 95% confident that the true failure rate for all components lies within this calculated interval.
